verdicts.note: The record marks 'yes' with a URL to https://docs.ecwid.com/api-reference, but this is simply Ecwid's REST API documentation, NOT a dedicated Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. The evidence shows only general API docs; no MCP server is demonstrated.
The URL points to Ecwid's own API reference page, not an MCP server repository or implementation. An MCP server would be a separate tool/integration, not the app's native API docs.
Evidence from https://support.ecwid.com/hc/en-us/articles/207100729-Ecwid-plans-and-features confirms 'REST API access requires a paid plan on standard tiers,' aligning with 'paid-plan'.

The first evidence page describes getting a custom app and secret tokens directly from the dashboard (self-generated), and the flags note developers can email support for a free upgrade. This is consistent with the note that paid plans are required unless manually requesting a developer upgrade.

Evidence clearly documents OAuth2 flow (https://docs.ecwid.com/get-started/make-your-first-api-request mentions OAuth 2.0 for public apps) and direct Token method (secret token copied from dashboard for custom apps). The March 2025 changelog confirms Bearer tokens in Authorization header are now the only way.
All evidence pages refer to Ecwid REST API v3; https://docs.ecwid.com/api-reference explicitly states 'Ecwid REST API allows you to access and manage any store data...'
https://docs.ecwid.com/api-reference lists endpoints for products, orders, customers, categories, discounts, store profile, inventory, etc., confirming a broad API surface.

Record states 'buildable-with-caveats' due to paid-plan requirement or manual email request for developer upgrade. Evidence supports this: free tier cannot access API (paid plan needed), though developers can request a free upgrade via support.

The blocker 'API access requires a paid Ecwid plan (or a manually-requested free developer upgrade via emailing support)' is directly confirmed by the evidence on plan requirements and the flags noting manual request needed.

Flags accurately reflect: no official first-party MCP server is shown in evidence; free API access requires manual email (not self-serve); and the first evidence page states 'There is no test mode in Ecwid API, so we recommend setting up a separate test store,' confirming no sandbox mode.

revised.name: Ecwid
revised.one_liner: Ecwid provides a broad REST API (v3) secured via OAuth2 or dashboard-issued secret tokens, but full API access typically requires a paid plan or a manually-requested developer upgrade.

auth_methods.notes: Standard OAuth 2.0 flow for external/public apps; exchange code for access token via POST to https://my.ecwid.com/api/oauth/token. Bearer token required in Authorization header; query param tokens disabled as of March 2025.
For custom, single-store apps, a pre-generated secret access token can be copied directly from the app dashboard, skipping the OAuth flow.

revised.self_serve_notes: Standard free-tier Ecwid stores cannot access the REST API; a paid plan is normally required. However, developers building integrations/public apps can request a free upgrade to a paid test store by emailing Ecwid API support, after which tokens can be self-generated via the app dashboard.

api_surface.notes: Ecwid REST API v3 covers products, orders, customers, inventory, store profile, etc.

revised.main_blocker: API access requires a paid Ecwid plan (or a manually-requested free developer upgrade via emailing support), which limits pure self-serve onboarding for agent toolkits.

evidence.supports: Describes OAuth2 flow and direct secret token access via app dashboard for custom apps.
Confirms Bearer token in Authorization header is now the only supported auth method.
Indicates REST API access requires a paid plan on standard tiers.
REST API overview showing broad API surface across store resources.

flags: Third-party MCP integrations (viaSocket, Vinkius) exist but no official first-party Ecwid MCP server confirmed.
Free API access for developers requires manual email request to support, not fully self-serve.
No sandbox/test mode exists; must use a real (paid or upgraded) store for testing.
